did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically a multi-model ResNet (M-ResNet) architecture and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), to detect skin cancer. It employs preprocessing, data augmentation, and classification on datasets like ISIC 2018, HAM10000, and PH2 to identify various skin cancers including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is presented at the 11th International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET) and describes original experimental work. It proposes a specific multi-model ResNet (M-ResNet) architecture, details a methodology involving preprocessing and data augmentation on datasets like ISIC 2018, HAM10000, and PH2, and reports original performance results (e.g., 95.7% accuracy for Inception V3, 99.30% for DenseNet 201) compared to dermatologists and other models. Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to present new technical results and validation of a proposed system.

title: Ensembled Deep Learning (DL) Methods for Detecting Skin Cancer Using CNN Algorithm with Multi Model
journal: 11th International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
brief_summary: This study investigates the use of ensembled deep learning methods, specifically a multi-model ResNet (M-ResNet) architecture, for detecting various skin cancers including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma. The proposed system employs a pipeline involving preprocessing, data augmentation, and classification using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on datasets such as ISIC 2018, HAM10000, and PH2. The evaluation approach compares the performance of models like Inception V3, DenseNet 201, and ResNet 152 against dermatologists using metrics such as accuracy, AUC, and ROC. Key results indicate that the Inception V3 model achieved an overall accuracy of 95.7%, while DenseNet 201 reached 99.30% for specific classifications, outperforming dermatologists in some metrics. The findings suggest that the proposed multi-model deep learning system is effective for early skin cancer detection and diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, actinic keratosis, vascular lesions, melanocytic nevi, benign keratosis, atypical nevi, dermatofibroma).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a 'Learning Classification System (SC-LCS)' for skin cancer detection. It classifies 8 specific skin disorders using datasets like ISIC 2018, HAM10000, and PH2. The models (ResNet 152, DenseNet 201, Inception V3) output class predictions evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC, and ROC. The study distinguishes between benign and malignant lesions and identifies specific cancer types.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 dataset, a collection of dermoscopic skin lesion images used for skin cancer detection research.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (ResNet 152, DenseNet 201, Inception V3) with 1760 benign and 1773 malignant images in the training set (Note: Text states 1760 benign and 1773 malignant in training set, and 360 benign and 300 malignant in testing set, totaling 3533 training and 660 testing images).
use.validation: Used for validation with 10% of the data (specific count not explicitly separated from the 80/10/10 split description, but implied as part of the split).
use.testing: Used for testing with 360 benign and 300 malignant images.
use.other: Used to compare performance against dermatologists and other state-of-the-art methods.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.
To assess model discrimination ability via Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics, including Micro AUC and Macro AUC.
To ensure comparable parameters and account for variability in training; results from multiple runs (e.g., Run 1, Run 2, Run 3) were averaged to determine model stability and accuracy.
To separate data for model training, verification, and final testing, ensuring generalization assessment.
To benchmark the deep learning models' performance (AUC ROC) against human experts.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, AUC, and ROC metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018.notes: The testing set contained 360 benign and 300 malignant images. The training set contained 1760 benign and 1773 malignant images. DenseNet 201 achieved the greatest Micro AUC (98.79%) and Macro AUC (98.16%). Melanoma detection accuracy was 92.18%. Dermatologists' AUC ROC values for Basal Cell Carcinoma and Melanoma were 88.82% and 82.26% respectively. The proposed system outperformed dermatologists by 11%.

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved 98% accuracy on the PH2 dataset. For HAM10000, the ResNet-CNN algorithm provided high-quality separation display. The paper mentions 10,015 images were employed in the study.

PH2.notes: The model achieved the highest classification accuracy of 90.67% on the PH2 and HAM10000 datasets combined in one statement, but specifically notes 98% accuracy for PH2 with high-quality separation display. 120 images were employed.

ISIC 2019.notes: The developed ResNet framework beat other techniques with this accuracy rate.

ISIC 2020.notes: The developed ResNet framework beat other techniques with this accuracy rate.

Comparison with Dermatologists.notes: The proposed models outperformed dermatologists. DenseNet 201 achieved 99.30% and ResNet 152 achieved 94.40% for specific classifications.

Hyperparameter Sensitivity (ISIC Dataset.notes: Results from multiple runs were averaged. Table 4 shows different batch sizes with learning rate 1e-4.

Literature Comparison (ISIC 2018).notes: Comparison of additional designs on ISIC2018 dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-Model ResNet (M-ResNet) architecture, specifically evaluating ResNet 152, DenseNet 201, Inception V3, and ResNet50. The study also proposes a combined ResNet-CNN and Inception-ResNet framework.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning strategy used. Inception V3 was trained on over a million images from the ImageNet database. Fine-tuning was applied to enhance accuracy.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224x3 for the proposed CNN model. 299x299 for the Inception framework.

training_details.learning_rate: 1e-4 (1106 mentioned in text likely typo for 1e-4 or similar, Table 4 specifies 1e-4)

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: 6.97 million parameters for the ResNet-CNN model

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing (to 224x224x3 for CNN, 299x299 for Inception)
Data augmentation including rotation, scaling, reflection, and shifting
Image enhancement using ESRGAN (Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks)
Transfer learning with fine-tuning of pre-trained networks (e.g., ImageNet)

conclusions: The proposed multi-model ResNet (M-ResNet) architecture and Skin Cancer Learning Classification System (SC-LCS) effectively detect various skin cancers, including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma.
The Inception V3 model achieved an overall accuracy of 95.7% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming other tested models such as ResNet 152 (94.40%), ResNet50 (83.60%), and the proposed CNN (83.10%).
DenseNet 201 achieved the highest classification accuracy of 99.30% and the greatest Micro AUC (98.79%) and Macro AUC (98.16%) for general classification tasks.
The deep learning models outperformed dermatologists by 11%, with dermatologists achieving AUC ROC values of 88.82% for basal cell carcinoma and 82.26% for melanoma.
Melanoma detection accuracy was significantly improved to 92.18% using the proposed system.
The ResNet-CNN algorithm achieved 98% accuracy on the PH2 dataset and 90.67% on the combined PH2 and HAM10000 datasets, demonstrating high-quality separation display.

Preprocessing techniques, including image resizing, normalization, and data augmentation (rotation, scaling, reflection, shifting), along with transfer learning and fine-tuning, enhanced model generalization and accuracy.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding vulnerability to adversarial attacks and potential bias, recommending future research to adopt standardized methods, transparent reporting, and the inclusion of both positive and negative datasets to improve reliability.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges limitations regarding vulnerability to adversarial attacks and potential bias in the models.
The authors recommend future research to adopt standardized methods and transparent reporting to improve reliability.
The paper suggests the need for inclusion of both positive and negative datasets to enhance model robustness.
Further research is needed to assess the system's effectiveness on larger datasets beyond those used in the study.
The study highlights the gap in research regarding the practical deployment of these models in real clinical conditions compared to detection performance.
